speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Byers Sanderson took place at his home near Bovill on October 16, 1975. Byers' brother, John Sanderson, was also present and participated in part of the conversation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father decided to come out here in the first place in his country.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: My father?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. Well, he came out, he was a sawmill man. When he came out here, he was running a sawmill for, he had a sawmill down to Kendrick. Then from Kendrick, he moved up to Princeton and he saw the timber for the Princeton when they was building. By the way, there were two buildings still standing, that big red barn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then the big house, you start up the hill, is there going out of Princeton there? Which way, towards Harvard or towards Potlatch?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Potlatch. Oh yeah, that's right up at Hampton. Yeah, that big house, he saw that lumber. Then he located up on, they called it the Beeson Meadows there at that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Then they left the Beeson Meadows and went over on Rock Creek out of Potlatch. Then they come into this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was working with a sawmill all the time?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, he had the sawmill himself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it was his mill?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he bring it out from the Midwest?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No. No, I think he bought it from a fellow named Horskins, the old Horskins sawmill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of size was that? What kind of mill was it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It was a circle saw. It's a good deal, them old -fashioned mills, you know.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Then he sawed the timbers for the potlatch coming in here.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He sawed the timbers for all these trussles and things coming in on there. He sawed them. He sawed them. But then he went assistant superintendent for the potlatch here when they first started. Him and old Tilly Pelton. He became an assistant superintendent for them. Tilly was a camp foreman and his dad was a foreman over all the camps. He had the shops. Then he had 14 donkeys to look after.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Then he had done all the ordering in the warehouse and run the shops. They run him to death.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He was pretty busy. He'd get up lots of times at four o 'clock in the morning to go up and get his bookwork and stuff done.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He was—well, he had that big warehouse. He had a hundred thousand dollar stock that he had to keep up. Then he had all the shop work too. That made lots of work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he expected to go out there and check on the foreman and see how the camps was being done at the same time?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He had these donkeys and things. They'd break down. Somebody seemed to be at that time, he was about the only man that was in the country to handle things like that, to break them up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He started at four.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did he get home?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, he—heh, it was all ours. Lots of times he'd work till nine, ten o 'clock at night. There in the office, they had an office in the shops. He'd work to get the books caught up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have a kind of pay to go along with that kind of responsibility?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He got a good wage. Yes, he got some. He had a good money at that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He—they was—at that time, the old men got all of their clothes and German rubbers, socks and jackets and mac and old pants.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He got that for free?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, they got that, you see, being a foreman. All the foremen got them at that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They—old Jimmy Costello would run the warehouse and Dad went down one time to get his clothes. Old Jimmy was a kind of a peculiar old fella. He didn't feel just right. He'd get pretty ornery. Dad told him what he wanted. Well, he says, I'm not going to get him to you today. Well, Dad says, I want him today. No, he says, I'm not going to get him to you. Well, he says, I'll go CTP.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He went out, started to go out, and Jimmy says, oh, he says, Charlie, wait a minute here. He says, come back here. He says, I'll get him to you.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, Dad says, all right. He'd get him to him. Them old fellas sometimes, they was pretty hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who was your father responsible to?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Was it T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones or was it Derry? Which one of them guys was he who recorded it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, it was T .P. Jones then. Dad was T .P.'s right -hand man for years there. That is after they started.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You see, they had their shop. They had five marians,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: four chaise,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: 14 or 21 donkeys,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: and then they had the chaise burnt the slabwood at that time. They never had coal. Later years, they got coal, but they had they. You'd see them old chaise coming along and see the sparks going 100 feet in the air, cinders. Then we made a respirator and put on the front, and it caught the sparks on the count of four spires. They'd set fires a dozen a day, but they had to have somebody put them out right along, faltered the engines right up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this, the equipment you put on, how did that work? The new stuff you put on to stop the sparks? Did you just put a top on it or what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, it was a big cone. The sparks come up in the smoke stack and they'd hit this cone and they'd whirl them. Whirl them, and then they had the screen. Then on the outside of this cone, they'd drop down on the inside of the stack. Then every so often, they'd pull the plug out and stop and clean the spark outfits out. They'd be out when they'd get out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had to have a man going along with each train just to put these spires out?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, no, they kept track of the sparks. If they started the fire, they'd just phone. They had a woods phone. They'd phone and tell them where the fire was and they'd put it out. Sometimes they'd follow up with one of these hand scooters that'd pump back and forth.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd put the fires out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that made your father decide to come to this Idaho country in the first place? Did he, had he heard about the potlatch coming out or how did he decide to come?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, no, he was a miner too, a mining engineer. He'd come out and he'd done lots of mining around like the Buffalo hump. He was in the Buffalo hump there. I think it was 1893, along in there. The person wasn't the person at that 93.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He followed the mining on a side issue. Then in 1904, he discovered the copper cane here in the hoodoos.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: That's the place they got that rich ore out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: A few years ago, these college kids went in and relocated it that he had dug out. They made $30 ,000.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: When dad had it, it was 20 miles into Harvard and there wasn't no railroad or anything to ship out ore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he ever tell you how he discovered it? How it came about getting in there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he work it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes, they worked it for years. T .P. Laird was in with him. Old Middle Dairy was in with him.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: After Bill died, Laird took over.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Laird went in with him. They kept it for years. Then the conditions were so that they couldn't get their ore out. They just throwed it up and let it go. Then this ore was laying there that they had hand -picked. That's when them college kids come over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just found the ore right on the ground?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, they had it piled up as big as this room.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They come in and just located it. Went and the railroad then was into Harvard, you see, up to Bovill here. It had been for years. They hauled out the high grade ore and checked it out and they made $30 ,000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father work this mine himself? Did he hire people to do that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They had five or six men working there for 20 years.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They got this high grade out of what they called the blossom. That's the peak of the mine. It was leached out from below in the ore body and deposited in what they called the blossom.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The prices weren't good enough to make it worth their while to take that pile out in the first place. No, at that time it wasn't. But no, that high grade ore that they had there, it had paid off handsome.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, as far as the company first coming out here, the Potlatch Lumber Company, what did you hear about how they found this country in the first place?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, old Bill Derry is the one that found the timber.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: When they was back, I guess it was Cloquet, Minnesota.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The warehouses, they'd buy when they'd earn any money, make any money, why they'd put it into timber back there. They bought all the timber that they could get. Then when Bill Derry told him, he'd work for them back there. He says, I know where the finest white pine timber that there is in the world is known. They wanted to know where it was. Well, he says, I'll tell you where it is, but I want to be in on it. He says, if I'm not in on it, I'll get it myself. Well, they jumped at the chance and they come out and they took saddle horses and come up and they come up on top of Beals Butte. They looked the country over and old Charlie Warehouser says, we want the whole thing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, they got a lot of it. They got it for practically nothing, you know. I don't remember what they paid for it, but it wasn't much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they get it? Who did they get it from?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They bought it from the state, I guess. They'd have to be the state that they bought it from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you learn that about Bill Derry telling him about this country?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, Derry used to come up and talk with Dad lots. And he'd tell Dad different stories and things about...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, the Warehousers said they were just common people. They was all business, but at the same time, why they had a different class of men than they are today. They had to find a good man. Well, they wasn't afraid to make it known. They'd put him in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Now, like, they'd come out and if they was doing a good job or something, why they'd go and buy him a good suit or something nice and give it to them. That is the old system of having to pay off, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you say they are common, you mean they were still close enough to being poor themselves that they knew what it was?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, yes. When they started out, back east there, they had even deprived themselves of stuff to buy timber.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They used to be a man here. I don't remember what his name was. I'd know it too if I'd hear it. It was raised back there and they'd buy oranges to eat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The warehouses, my land, what do you buy oranges for? Why don't you put your money into timber?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, they were skidding it back there, probably a dollar an acre.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But they were that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They put every nickel that they could rake and scrape.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Then, later years, he told this fellow, I wished I could think of his name. He was an old man when he was out here. I know him well. I used to like to hear him tell the stories. He'd laugh. The warehouses would laugh and shake their finger at him and say, I told you, when you were spending your money to put it into timber, now he says, I can buy oranges and you have to go with a halt.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, he was right. They made their money right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the laws in those days about buying and cutting timber were such that a family like the warehouses could just start with about nothing and just cut and go?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The laws at that time, they never paid much attention to the timber.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You could buy timber for practically nothing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And everybody would go in the woods and cut their wood. There was nothing ever said. They'd even cut good tamarack trees and make cord after cord and pile it up and let it season.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They never had any laws regarding the timber. The first fire patrols that they had in here to watch the timber was a fellow named Fritz Lindhart, who used to be at Harvard. He had an old motorcycle. He'd go out on this motorcycle. Of course, there was only just little old pack trails through the country and that's what he'd do. He'd go up the pack trails. But if he had a forest fire, they would be lucky if they got anybody to fight it because there was nobody in the country to fight it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just rode around looking for him?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, yes. He might have well saved a hunter because he'd had a certain trail that he'd go. He'd go up into the hoodoos and then he'd come over and take the trail down here at Harvard.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: This little old town used to be up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Using the caws or the slab towns?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He'd come on through and go on the trail and go back over to Harvard where he lived. That is about his territory. But there wasn't any fire patrol that they got today at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he on the company payroll?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, he was on the payroll at the potlatch. That's after they had bought the timber, most of the timber in here. Then they bought lots of the old homesteads that was here. They bought them. Fellas had proved up on their homesteads and the potlatch had bought them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they were reluctant to sell or were most of these guys just not to get it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. They'd sell out for practically nothing. There was lots of snow at that time. Of course, where they had the meadows, there was a lot of those old fellows that stayed right there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Most of the old homesteaders sold out the potlatch after the potlatch got to go on a little bit.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Today, they owned 90 % of all of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the land and through this country? What about the Forest Service?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, there wasn't any Forest Service then. But now the Forest Service, they took over lots of the land. I don't know how the Forest Service took over their ground, but you see the railroads that come through the country. They had given thousands of acres that there wasn't entitled to at all to get the railroads to come through.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they're having trouble over that even yet today.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They took it up and tried. I don't know whether they got any of it back or not, but now you take the Milwaukee land.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: All the railroads that come through the country, they had given thousands of acres to get their roads through. Of course, they had millions of acres then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what the thinking was about building a whole company town there at Potlatch along with the mill?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, they built the town thereself, the Potlatch did. And they rented their houses out to the people that worked in the mill after the mill got started. By then, they built the town. And the store and everything was run by the Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they figure that they'd make money that way, or did they just do it to have a place for the people who worked in the mill?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, they made money there. That way, after they'd built the town, the store was a Potlatch store, and you'd buy your groceries while they'd just hold it out of your check.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And then the houses, they never rented for much. I think it was ten, twelve dollars a month or something like that.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And lots of times, the fellows would want to do buildings theirself onto the houses that they were renting.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They were a good company to help the working man at that time. They've always been a good company to work for.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Even when the wood was here, the families would come in. They wouldn't have any saws or anything. They'd give them a crosscut saw and a axe and a shovel and split them off to cut their own wood. Well, they'd done that to get the people to stay in and work.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I didn't know that they did that.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They were very good that way.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I've heard in Potlatch they would let people charge the store right away once they went to work for them. Oh, yeah. You came in with nothing. Yeah. You could come in, move right into one of their houses. If you're going to work for the company, you could move right in. They'd take everything right out of their check and give them credit, which was a godsend to the people at that time. At that time, if you earned G400 dollars a year, it was doing good.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You'd take in the Blue's Country in the early day. The harvest in Gatherin was the main support of the whole country.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The merchants, old Fredericks and Fuzzy, would carry families through the whole year. Then when they got their harvesting gathering, they'd go in and pay up their bills.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Of course, you could buy a different than you do today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this Fredericks that had?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He was just in Palouse. He was a big grocery man there. This would be really up until Potlatch came. Even after the Potlatch got their mill built there at Potlatch, the storekeepers there in Palouse would carry them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd come in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The people I think were different in those days. They owed you a dollar, they'd come and pay it. They never lost any money. They were only by charging accounts.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The same way at Potlatch, when the harbor got started by the little store there run by the Smiths, he always carried all the settlers in there. Sometimes he'd carry them in their ear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it that the storekeeper knew the people a lot better, knew them personally? Was that why he'd trust them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It probably was. They was homesteading.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: What little work they'd get, they'd go in and pay on their bills.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But Flory Smith, he was in the courthouse in later years. But in his home there, he had a little store.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Like the mine, they'd go down there. They'd get $75 or $100 worth of groceries. Never thought about paying for them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd take every 30, 60 days. They'd go in and pay up. But the whole thing was run on credit.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they made money. Of course, the dollar at that time was worth a good many times what it is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did your father first go to work for Potlatch? Was it when they were building out through dairy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, dad went to work for the Potlatch back in 1903, I believe it was, in 1904.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And that's when the mining first started up in the country.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And he'd work up at Harvard. You see, at Harvard, the railroad wasn't in there then. The railroad never went in until 1906, but at Harvard, they'd footlogging the dump. It was done by the people that had horses. And they'd pull their logs into the river, and then they'd flown down to Palouse in the spring, to the Palouse Mill. And then after they got the railroad up as far as Harvard, then they'd put in a big camp there at Harvard and log that country in there. But they floned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this cod? Was this cod or was this potlatch?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They were here then, they were logging before 1906, before they built the mill. Yeah, before they got the mill, you see, they was cutting their timber at the Palouse Mill. I think old McCormick is the one that owned the mill down there then. He'd cut their timber for them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old McCormick was, he was a mill man.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They had, in Fluming, they had dams every so often. Oh, like at Harvard, they'd have a dam, and they'd hold water until they'd get a big body of water, and then they'd turn it loose on logs and wash them on down to Princeton, and they had that, they worked that way all the way down to Palouse.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And at Palouse, they had a big dam. They'd let the logs fill up in there, and then they'd get the men and open up the dam, and they'd shoot them through the dam on down, the water would carry them on down to the mill.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I've got pictures, I don't know where they are, I was going to look them up, of some of the logs that the old town was, oh, they were for a mile, a mile and a half up the river. I've seen a picture of the looses that just covered with logs on the river. And then after they got the mill at Potlatch,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: and the railroads at Harvard, they brought the logs down to Harvard and cut them there at Potlatch. But they flew them here before the railroad got up to here. You see, the railroad never got up here until 1907, I believe it was. And they'd had a dam up here at Slabtown, and they'd put them in there, and then they'd plume them on down to the Red Dam down below here. Oh, it's about four miles down below, and then they had a big dam there, and the railroad, they'd had the railroad in 1906 up to there,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: and they'd pick them out of the pond, roll them onto their train, take them to Potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To what point was this at where they had the railroad?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The old Red Dam, they called it the Old Red Dam.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is that located?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It's about two, three miles below the bowl here. It's part of the old dams there yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the flume construction like in those early ones?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, they never had, they never built a flume like they did in later years. They'd just hold them to the creek, and they'd had a crew. They'd go through, and if any trees had crossed the creeks or anything, they'd cut them out. So they'd be clear, and they'd just, in the high water, they'd hold this water back, and then they'd turn the water loose, and then they'd go along, and if they hung up anywhere, they'd had a big pipe pole and a peavey, and they'd roll them into the creek again, and away they'd go. In other words, the water did all the work. The water done all the work, yeah. For them getting them on the table. Outside of, if they'd hang up or anything, they'd take the peaveys and follow the drive right down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the creek very big?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, not necessarily, no.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: In later years, of course, the creek's filled up with brush and stuff going along, but when they'd turn that water loose, they'd, oh, the creek would be as wide as this room, and they'd float them right on down. There was no trouble in floating them. They had to do this in high water. Oh, yes. Yeah, they'd done lots of it in the high water, but that's the reason they built the dams, to make the high water. They'd see the creek sprung at that time, year -round.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: After they cut the timber off, while it didn't have no watershed, they quicks dried up practically in the summer. But at that time, they quicks run year -round. So they would have log bex all along the creek, waiting to roll the logs into them. Yeah, wherever they was logging. Now, here in this country, here, when they'd took all the, anything was close to the river with their teams, and they'd pull the logs over to, and they were short logs. There wasn't long logs. And they'd pull them to the dam, roll them into the dam, and then when they got a head of water up, why, they'd open up the dam and shoot them down to this other dam, you see, in the same way it blew us there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they pull all the logs up at the dam, or did they also have them stacked along the river itself?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, they'd pull them into the dam, and they'd log just around the dam. Of course, the timber come right down to the edge of the creek's dam. They was beautiful, being quite fine. Sam Frye, up at Collins there, he had his homestead, but the Frye boys all had their homesteads, and they got $4 .00 a thousand with a potlatch after the railroad first got in here, $4 .00 a thousand for their timber, and they had to cut it and haul it to the landing for $4 .00 a thousand.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Now, you couldn't even, you couldn't look at it at all. It cost you, I suppose, around $60, $70 .00 a thousand just for the timber stumpage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year do you think they started logging in here for the company?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They started in 19, 6, I believe, 5 or 6 up here. They started in 19, 3 or 4 there at Harvard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They flew there at Harvard, you know, to the railroads, and they got the mill built, and I think they built the potlatch mill, it was built in about 1906, wasn't it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I know the railroad started in 1906 to go up to potlatch.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: So the railroad started before that, the railroads started in 5, yeah, 4 and 5, but they never got it into potlatch till... That was all done by handwork, too, at that time, in teamwork.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They didn't have, I think it was done by flips. It seems to me like it was flips.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The team would pull this flip, you know, they'd blast the rock down, then they'd go in and haul the rock out and make the railroad bed.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They was lots of men at that time. When they put that railroad in, it must have been 200, 300 men working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd they come from?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The most of them were Italians, and they could get them to work for practically nothing. And they were powder men.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They claimed to be powder men at that time.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: With powder men. Well, dynamite. They used dynamite.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Most of them came into the country just right then. They hadn't been living here anything. No, they brought them in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: When those railroads come in, they're in the place where they was... They'd go out and work, blast.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd get a rock too big or anything to handle it. They'd drill it and blow it apart. It was all handwork. And later, they did it. Later, they had old shovels, great big fellas that would... Well, they looked big as a locomotive at that time. They brought them in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They was a wooden structure over the machines, but them old dippers had stuffered, run out like a shovel. But they were crude, but they'd done the work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was after the railroad had been built, though, the main railroad?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, most of it, yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You said that Helmer was the first big camp that they had on this side of the... No. No, Harburg. I mean over on this side, not over...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the Blue side?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. I was thinking over in the dairy... Well, yeah. Helmer was about 19 -6 and 7. The potlatch put up a big camp there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Dad had the sawmill, and he cut the timber for the railroad to come on in. From Harburg on, you see, they had to have timbers to go across, and he cut... That's what he was doing, was cutting out the timbers for them. They had the sawmill set right in the gully below Helmer there now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they'd bring the logs into the landing by team, and then they had the camp, until the pelton run the camp. And that was the first big camp they had. How many men were in the camp when... Just roughly. 75 -80 men there then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was most of the lumber that they were cutting going to make these timbers at the time?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. It was making lumber for bringing the railroad in, and they was building the camps. They used it in building their camps, too. Now, they had wagons, and they'd load a wagon up with lumber, and they'd haul it clear through up to here. And then Sam Fry got the idea of putting in a little mill himself. And he wouldn't finish the lumber. It'd be just cut off in slabs. Rough, sometimes the bark would still be on the lumber. But they built Slabtown out of... That's where it got its name, was just Slabwood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Slabwood cut by Sam Fry's mill?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But Camp 8, Camp 8 was built by the lumber that they sold at Helmer. They brought it in by wagon.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But Sam Fry, he built Slabtown, and then he sold the lumber for most of the baubles at that time to build. You could buy it for nothing.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, this was Camp 6, the one at Helmer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that what it was called then?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, yeah. Old Camp 6 wasn't the new Camp 6. It was old Camp 6.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You lived there when you were a kid, is that right?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, yes, I played there. We lived in Palouse, but we'd come up every year and stay there, camp out and stay.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Dad did come home every evening, you see, in the summer.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And that's one thing old Tilly Pilton, at that time, the farmers, there wasn't farmers then, they were settlers. Around, they'd come for, the kids would come for miles around to see the logon camp. They'd stand outside, they could smell the old cookhouse, you know, you could smell the cakes and stuff they was making. And old Tilly had come up, he'd come on boys, come on, take them into the cookhouse. The cook, he says, picks them up something to eat. And he'd go and get the meat and cookies and cakes and pies, put them out there, and they'd, every one of them, eat till they'd put their pop open.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dad asked Tilly one time, he had amused Dad, and Dad said, Tilly, how is it that you gather up all of these kids that come up around here and take them into the camp to something to eat?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old Tilly looked over and he says, I'll tell you Charlie, he says, I was a kid once myself and he says, I was always hungry.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He was a good old fella. He told me one time, we was packed up and ready to go home and they had a lot of little pigs. He says, buyers, he says, if you want one of those little pigs. He says, when the wagon comes by, you get them herded up by the road here, and when the wagon comes by, you grab one, get on to the wagon. He says, the old sow will take after you when he squeals. So I heard it all day till the wagon, the folks come by and I grabbed one of the little pigs and he says, weeee! The old sow took after me and boy, I just barely did get on the wagon too now. But I took that pig home and cleared a place. I took care of him till we got home and we raised him. We commenced to misten the milk, our cows.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: One morning, we didn't know why the milk could be gone every morning. They'd go out to milk and she'd be dry. We looked out one morning and here sat this pig sitting up there and he was sucking the cow and she was standing there chewing her caw. Well, we kept the pig in the pen after that.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Finally, we let the pig out and he'd come and watch Mother. She was making preserves and stuff. She had a big dishpan full of preserves. He'd lay his head at the door and she'd go into the front room and he'd run around to the other door. But he wouldn't come in the house. One day, they ordered flour and stuff from the delivery. He'd come up once a week and he brought a sack of flour and set it down at the table. She was making these preserves that day. She went to town and once you come back, the delivery wagon had come up and set this flour down at the table. Never put it up at all. That's the way they put the groceries and he put the groceries up on the table and the flour down there. Well, this blooming pig got in the house and he rooted this sack of flour open. He had it all over and he had the pan of preserves. He stood up and rooted the preserves off of there and he had the awfulest mess that you ever seen. They were cracked right into the front room and threw into the bedroom. Mother went in there and here laid that pig. He had his head on the pillow all over flour and preserves. Well, that was the end of the pig. They butchered him right away.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I laughed.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Mother laughed too. It was disgusting but she had laughed at the blooming pig. She said, well, that's what you get for stealing a pig. I didn't steal it until they give it to me.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But that was a...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Me too, like probably in those days there wasn't much of a way for a lot of women if they were single. No. Unless they could be a school teacher or a cook there wasn't too many other things they could do. No, they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: There wasn't no jobs then to mount anything.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Outside the housekeeping. They never got no nothing for housekeeping. Do you think any of these girls ever saved up enough money to get out of the business and go, Oh no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Become respectable?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No. No, they spent their money as fast as they got it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They have madams too, like you hear about now. A madam for the house, you know. A lady that run the place.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, the lady of the house. She took her egg off of all the girls. That's how they run the house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want a cup of coffee?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you here at the same time, bars, at that fire?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you right here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes. I was running the stationary engine up Camp 8 in the pumping system. I was the last one that left Camp 8. Camp 8 in the burning. I went out and dropped the hose, fixed the... Well, I took a cross like that, put the hose and the wire onto the side of the shop, and turned the pumps on where I left. Then I come down the railroad track and come across from the railroad track over across here. There was woods clear across here then.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: When the fire was starting, I blowed the whistle.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I pulled the whistle to warn the people that it was crowning and coming over fast. Boy, there was chunks of wood that long. That big around just sailing right through the air, a fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. They'd go... They'd go two, three hundred yards and drop. They'd set another fire. That's why it travels so fast. Chokes of wood maybe two, three feet long and six, eight inches wide. Limbs, see limbs of trees just at the top of the butte. The wind come up on one side and the fire on the other, and when they met, it twisted off trees that big around, just twist them off like matches. It's terrible. And the clouds come over, camp eight there in Slabtown, black from crowning, and it just exploded out here. Just a big flash. It was gas, what it was, gases. And it set fires all along in there. And boy, I'll tell you, it killed horses, cows, chickens, pigs, everything.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, when did you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fires, when did you first know that the fire was on its way? How much warning did you have?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, boy. It roared like a thousand freight trains.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And it come.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It was an awful roar.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: When it first started, the potlatch said it, was burning the slashes, which they done every year. But they come down and they told Jones that the fire was getting away from them. You'd better get some more men onto it. He says, let her burn. He says, that'll clean it up. They cleaned it up. But it got away from them entirely then.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: So did they actually let it burn in a while because Jones said... Well, yes. They didn't try to control it then. They didn't know what was going to bust out like it did. But when it broke out by... I'll tell you, it was a terrible thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How fast did it move then when it broke away? It came off of Beel's Butte?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yep, up by Beel's Butte. And the fire went up Beel's Butte and also this way, you see. Oh, it traveled faster than a horse could run. After it got out here, it just traveled terrible fast.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It was a terrible thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you remember where you were when you first found out about it? Were you at home or were you at the shop?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You could hear it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You could hear it. They all left the shop. Yeah. And come down. But I stayed there for quite a while. Women had... After they'd all gone and turned the water pumps, I had the water pumps and stuff turned on to the shop.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just soaking the shop down?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But the hill there saved the shop. The fire went over the top.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: The fire, say that cloud, jumped clear over the shop and lit down on the other side and started to race up through the woods then. When I come down home here, it was all the fire all in here. Up there, it burnt that laundry out that used to be up there. It burnt all the slab town out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It burnt the whole town? The slab town?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It killed chickens and pigs up there at slab town. They all had pigs and...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Chickens. Chickens.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much warning did the people at slab town have?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They didn't have much. They got their trunks packed for their... The engine was up at Collins.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Up into the camps and they come down. Old Tarbox was running 23 and the twins, his wife had twins that morning.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They wanted him to stay there. But he says, you fellas can stay here if you want to, but he says I'm taking the engine going into Bogle.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He came in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they want to stay there? Was it too much fire on the track?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, he was worrying about his family. No, but I mean on the... The other fellas wanted to get out of there. In case they had to get out of there, they wanted the engine and flat cars to be there to pull them out. But he says I've got a wife and baby's there. And he says I'm not staying here and let them get a chance of burning up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: So he took the train... He was the engineer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was the train at?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Up at... Up above Collins there, one of the camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he pick up people as he came in?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, he picked up people as he came through. But when he came through Camp 8, I'll tell you, he was traveling. They told him the bridge was on fire, the railroad bridge. And that made him all the worse. He opened it up and boy, he went across that bridge miles an hour. He came down to Bogle here and they picked the flat cars up and loaded the people in town. And everybody that wanted to got on there and the way they went to Potlatch. And the bookkeeper of Camp 8 got on to Potlatch and he had a little fox terrier dog, old Jack Donovan. And he had this dog under his arm running around hunting for his wife. And his wife hadn't got on the train. She was up here in Bogle. They had quite a story about that. He grabbed his pooch and let his wife go.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, it must have been terrible confusion. Oh, there was. There was. They come down. There was people down here that knew Dad and they run in and was lugging the furniture stuff out in the meadow here and it was going to leave it out in the meadow. Dad says, you just lug her back in the house and he says, she burns, we let her burn.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He says, he carried all the pieces anyhow, getting it out and in. He says, no use spoiling everything. He says, I ain't afraid, but we can save the house. Of course, the quick was running then. There was lots of water in the quick and they filled buckets up and had one up on the roof and pouring water. The sparks would light on the house and they poured water on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you did? Did you take part in that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, I didn't get down until I fired, kind of died down. I stayed up at the camp eight. I was here putting the fire out of my house. Dad was working on this and he was very on edge. The other one over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do? Did you stand on the roof and have somebody hand you buckets of water? Or what?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I ran down the ladder and get water.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, Dad had help there to get water out of the creek. I caught my wife on the train with her folks. I came back and when she got to Potlatch, found I wasn't there or she was mad.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Ha!

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Then at the Potlatch, Sam Fry went after the fire. He went after Jones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, what are you going to do about burning my houses up?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Jones says, I'm not going to do anything. Sam says, yes you are. He says, it's your fire and your fault. He says, my houses and stores and stuff burned up. He says, you're going to pay for them. He says, if you don't, I'm going to start suit on to you right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones says, what do you want for them? I think he said $2500, didn't he?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I don't remember. Yeah, I think it was $2500. Oh, he says, that's too much. He says, it ain't too much. I'll get the suit started against the Pottlehatch right away. He started out again and Jones says, well come back. He told the timekeeper to make him out of check for 2000. He knew that they had started the fire and they had proof of it and they were responsible for it. He paid him off. Mrs. Stockwell, he came down and he was talking with Mrs. Stockwell and she's the one that owned the laundry up here. She says, I can't get a thing out of them. Well, Sam says, you come go with me. She went down to the office and Jones happened to be in. He says, Mr. Jones, he says, what are you going to do about Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stockwell's laundry up there?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He says, you burnt that out. Well, he says, I can't do anything there. He says, we can't do anything. Well, he says, you are going to do something. He says, you're going to pay her for her house too. He says, I don't care about anybody else, but he says, her house, you're going to pay.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And he said, I'll give her $2000.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did Sam Fry care so much about her place?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Because it was her business that got burned out. He was just a friend. He was just a friend and Sam knew he had him over a barrel and he just pressed it for Mrs. Stockwell. They were old settlers in here together, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of proof did Sam have?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Did somebody... The camp, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The camp set the fire up there at camp 14, wasn't it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It might have been 14. It was back up that draw at the foot of Beals Butte.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They were burning the slasheins. And then old Sam and them heard him when they told him the fire was getting away. He heard, Jones says, let her burn.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: the people in Bovill think that the town was a goner. Oh, yeah. Yes. They all left. Most of them. The most of them. They took a load down the potlatch on the flat cars. They just grabbed what few belongings they had and left.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear the story that the engineer tried to get Tarbox to jump or stop and Tarbox threatened to hit him with a wrench? Did you ever hear that one?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I heard that story someplace. No, Tarbox didn't.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But I think he did come to think about it. I think he did tell them up there to Collins when they was going to stop him, not let him come. And he grabbed their ranch shoes. He tried to stop me and he says, I'll knock you in the head. And he got up on the engine and took off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it really that dangerous for him to bring the train down into Bovill at that point?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, the fire was burning the bridge then. And he pulled her wide open. I'll tell you, I seen that old engine when it went across there and drivers just like that. This bridge was at Camp E. Yeah, it was one of the things now. He had the whistle blowing to let him know that it was coming. And the little fireman, Dick Thomas, was his name, had his head out the window. Boy, he was just as white as a sheet. Scared to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were all the people from Slabtown on this train that he was taking?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. No, no, no. They had come on into Bovill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Already?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They walked in?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. Some of them had a lot of teams from the company, Bon. They were packing all the stuff out. So he was hauling mostly the men from... Then after they got to Bovill, they grabbed three, four flat cars more and loaded the people on from town and took them to Potlatch. By that time, the fire was burning even down by Harvard. It's 25 miles front.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Used to have some awful fires.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, I've waited.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They take us out of the shop. I've walked in the trails where the trails going in. Dust would be that deep, run just like water down the mountainside and the men are walking. The dust is running right ahead of them. Halfway up to your knee. Yeah. You couldn't tell a man of the cocoon or what he was when they'd get down.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They always grabbed me and put me in a camp of plunking.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: That's because I was a young kid, I guess.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They had a fire out here on McGarry Butte.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were in that too?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, I was just...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I had a tar box. I love them. They had me down watching the cars at the end of the horse ranch.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: After the fire broke away from him, my young Matthews, he come tearing out all by himself. Well, he says I got out, but I guess the rest of them are all burnt up. I walked in under a fire that time. It is crowning. I walked in under over 200 yards. All the fire above me, just like a big oven. Your head and hat and shoulders got so hot you could hardly stand it. An old tar box, the old engineer on the 1914 fire,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: was mopping his head with an old red bandana, and he had it wet. He was mopping his head as he was coming out. He stopped to rest. He said, go on, Byron, go on, go on, go on, get out of here. I said, I'm not going until you get out of here. He said, I'll get out all right. I said, yeah, and I'll go out with you. I said, I'm not going until you go. We were the last ones to get out of the fire. He was pretty old at that time. Everybody down below thought he was burnt up. They thought we were burnt up. Our wives and them was down there. Theirs was bald and squawking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it happen?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You guys got stuck behind the others. Well, the tar box couldn't be out on that. They went on a dead run. An old tar box got tired, and he couldn't make it. He stopped. I stopped with him. I was going to see that he got out. That's why the fire crowned over your head and got past you. Well, no, the fire crowned over the head when we come over the McGarry Butte there. You see, Frank Mallory, he led us right down in. Well, here's your gully. Here's a hogsback here and a hogsback here. We were going down this gully to make a trail down to get in to cut the fire off.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They was wind blowing then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says to old Frank Mallory, I says, it's pretty dangerous going down the head of this fire, isn't it? He says, what do you suppose they got me in here for?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He says, I know what I'm doing. Well, Ray Waldron, Kate's husband, says, I don't believe you do know what you're doing. He says, that fire crowns, he says, you're going to burn the whole bunch of us up. Just then, it started to roar. The men started to run it in every direction. Old Mallory's eyes got that big and he says, this way, this way, this way. Boy, they were going just like sheep.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He took off on a high lope just as hard as he could go to the top of the hill.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: We all was running then. We got over to the top of the hill and going down on the other side, that slowed up some. But it was crowning over us before we got up to the top and on the other side. The bottom of the trees wasn't burning then. It was just the wind was taking it right over the tops of the trees. It was blowing from one hog back to the other. Axel Anderson was the walker who was with us. I told him, I said, by gullies, I tried to tell Mallory that he was leading us into an awful trap the way he was doing. Yes, he says, I know it. He says, I thought of it too. He says, I will tell you, he won't be a foreman anymore, a fire warden anymore. Sure enough, they got rid of him. Never did put him back onto a fire warden.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He led us right into that trap, which is the biggest wonder in the world, that he didn't burn us up. Davis Fellows lost their jackets, took their jackets off. They got hot, and they took their jackets off so they could run faster.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Once you broke over to the other side, were the people safe?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, no. They had to run all the way down. There was fires all around them as they come through. You see, these sparks were going 100 yards ahead of you all the time, setting new fires, and they were as dry as powder. It took right off on high.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: You could hear that fire roar for a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter? Why was it that the guy couldn't judge something like that? You meant the men could see it, right? You could see the danger of getting in a place with a fire?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, he seen it too, but he was bullheaded, and he didn't want nobody to tell him anything.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: That was the way Mallory was. He was even that way up until he died. You couldn't tell him anything.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And then he left here, and he got a job as superintendent over in Montana there. But of course, but he didn't make good. No, no. He got into a bad over there, over the same thing, bullheaded. He wouldn't listen to anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, during that fire, what were you doing? You were down below?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, I was working up at Camp 8, the shop, and I come down. I wanted to get my wife out, and she lived wherever we are now.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I got her downtown on the train, left, and I come back up.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: There were 300 men that came up from Potlatch about the time I got here. They decided right out along the fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Right out in the meadow here?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They went along the side of the fire, kept it from coming this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about on that McGarry Butte fire? What were you doing then? Did his wife really think that he got killed in the fire?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They didn't know. They put me watching cars at the other end of the horse ranch. That was right at the foot of McGarry Butte. They were fighting fire then. And I saw that wind coming, and boy, that took the fire right away from them. It started to go, and I was fearful of what it might do to the men. I knew there was a lot of men out in there. Pretty quick, here come Delbert Matthews. He sure had to go over there. Well, he says to me, I got out, but I don't think the rest of them will. He says they'll be burnt up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He really thought that, huh?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, he really thought that. But here come, if you want, here come some more of them. Well, we got out. I don't know whether the rest will make it or not, but they kept coming. Tillie was tarboxing, buyers, it was after. They said I don't know whether old harvox will get out or not.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Hi, but Tillie doesn't sound like he was too hard on the men. Oh, no. No, Tillie was a good man. He wasn't a hard man at all for these men. And they all liked him. He was well -liked.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I guess Homer, I think it's Homer, had some pictures the last time I seen him. He showed them to me. I think it was 19...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, it must have been 1905, when the picture, I think Homer had it in 1904, but I don't believe it was 1905 that he had some pictures of old Camp 8, and they moved up from Camp 6. They moved up from Camp 6 and moved into one of the shacks that was built. I've seen those pictures, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's near Collins, right?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. He showed them to me when I was over once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the living quarters were at Camp 6, old Camp 6?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. They had shacks. Just shacks is all there was put up all around. Now, Tillie moved into one, and the other... The slab town, they had a dozen houses there, too. But all around Camp 8 there, they had little shacks. One room, sometimes they had two rooms. They'd cook and sleep all in the same room. Then they had the two big bunk houses. Then they had a big barn there. Then they had the cook house and cellars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this Camp 8 or Camp 8?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They had a lot of buildings there at that time. Of course, there wasn't any big buildings. They were just shacks put up for the accommodations. Then they had some tents put up, too, with walls around the foot of the walls.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But the main body of the men that was working, they stayed in the bunk houses. Then the horses, they had the big barn there. They had lots of the men even slept in the barn in the hay.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They had the dam. I used to go up in the evenings and watch them roll the logs in, them old jacks, and get out there, and they'd cuff them logs.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd roll the logs to see which ones stay on the longest.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It was more like a big family at that time. The lumberjacks, they all knew one another. They'd get out there and roll logs, have a high old time driving those logs.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd open the dam. It wasn't very steep, but the water, they'd come out and they'd go down. They'd jump on a big log and down through the chute and say, oh, lots of them would fall off, you know. But they never got hurt in any of them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd ride those logs. Some of them would ride them all for two, three hundred feet down the river, and then they'd jump off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they straddle the log and just hold onto their legs and stay on it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, they'd stand on it. Of course, they had the cork boots. They'd see which one could ride the furthest.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I used to get quite a kick out of watching them cuff the logs there. I'd get out there and, boy, now some of them logs, before they'd open the dam, they'd just sing. They'd go to one of them and be bucked off, and then somebody else would get on and go around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see them burrowing logs?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, but I've never seen anybody do it well. I've seen kids do it more than anything else. Yeah, there was old Jack's, boy, now I'll tell you. They was balanced at all time on them logs. Of course, they'd get right out into the pond and they'd go across the logs. The logs would hit and go down when their weight had gone on them, and yet they'd sail along them just like a fly.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Very seldom one ever went into the water. They was trained on that. That's what they'd done in the early days, in the log drives they'd ride the logs lots. Then if they'd see a log that was hung up on the bank, why, they'd jump off and wade ashore and kick it in.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: So here they were doing it for entertainment. Oh, yes, yeah, they got quite a kick out of it. But most of the burrowing was in the evenings after the work. As soon as they'd go down to the pond and get out there and burrow the logs to see who could stay on the longest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it tough on those guys?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I've heard that there was also quite a bit of fighting, especially when they drank. Yes, they always had a bully at the camp. Now, old Charlie White was the bully of the camp there when Pelton had the camp, until an old fella named Joe Rivers came in, in Cloquet, Minnesota. Joe was a big Indian, and he took over. But they'd battle one camp against the other.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, Joe Rivers, did he have to beat up Charlie White to become both of the... Oh, yes, he had to lick him. Yes, he had to lick Charlie to be called the Bullard Woods.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old Joe was a pretty skook of man. He lost his leg after he was out here a while. I don't know just how he lost it. But even with his leg off, he'd run, put one hand on the ground, turn a flip -flop near and light on his foot, the one foot. He was that super, you know. Did he have a wooden leg, or did he just... Oh, yes. He had an old wooden leg. He made it himself.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: He was an old blacksmith, and he made this leg himself. He made the men later on why he bought his legs. I think at that time they'd have to go to Spokane and go in and be fitted two, three times for a fellow up there who was making artificial limbs and legs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Joe Rivers keep working after he lost his leg?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes. Yeah, he worked up until he retired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he still do what was he restricted as far as the kind of work he could do compared to what he'd done before?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, he done blacksmithing. He went to blacksmithing helper later because he couldn't get around too good. He could stand, you see, and use a sledge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what he did before he got hurt? Was he blacksmithing before that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, yeah. He was blacksmith back in Cloquet, Minnesota, a bartender. He was a bartender, and then he went to the Logan Camps, why he always went in as a blacksmith. He got a little bit, ten cents an hour probably, more for a blacksmith than they did at the other work.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But he was a pretty good old blacksmith. He'd make old pipe pole spikes and hooks. He'd make ice tongs in the winter. He'd make ice tongs to put up ice with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's this idea of bullying in the woods, though? Does this mean that the bully of the woods, he's got to be able to take on all comers? Is that the idea?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Well, they did in the fighting. If he was fighting for the bully of the camp, it seemed to be that it was an honor, like a prize fighter today. It was an honor for him to be a bully of the woods. Then they'd have competition between camps.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The bullies from one of the bullies from the other?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yes, they'd fight like buzz saws. They were honorable in the fights when they licked the other fella and had him licked by it. They'd let it go with that. They'd be good friends.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But they, they.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: It was quite an honor to be a bully of the woods.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, that bully of the woods even went up to the time that Nogel took over as his superintendent here. And over at Moscow one time, they made a big cage and they put Nogel in it across the top of the cage, bully of the woods.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old Joe Rivers, he, he, they was two brothers of them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they was, when they got so old that they couldn't take care of themselves, right, they, they took him over to the county home.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old Joe lived, I think Joe lived till he was 90. Some of his brothers lived to 103, I think it was. They was old hard drinkers too.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They,

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: they.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Johnny is Grover. Johnny Grover was 103.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Joe Grover. He lived, put, put in there a hundred.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Do you think, you know, I know I read about like in the Midwest there was a lot of fighting that went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there, you think there was a lot here too among the lumberjacks, but would it only be like when they'd been drinking or how did that go?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, they, they, the, the bull of the woods, oh it is without a question, it was when they was drinking more than with fighting.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But for the, the bull of the woods, why a new man come into the camp and if he happened to challenge this other fellow at all, why there was a fight right now. They, they really battled. They never had no gloves or anything. They went at it till the other and hollered, one of the other hollered.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But they'd get drunk. That's a different thing. They'd, they was always a fight.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they were, they were tougher in those days than they are now. Of course they, these young kids, they get to drinking in there. They want to fight too, but they, more of a bluffing type. Those days why you had to fight if you're looking for trouble, why there was always somebody to accommodate them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They really went at it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember seeing fights when you were, when you were, when you were young?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yeah, I see lots of them. Always had a horror of it. And I've seen them, I've seen them at the Palouse when they'd come to Palouse, celebrate there. And there was 13 saloons in Palouse. I've seen them, the blood'd be just a spurtin' out of some of them. saloon fights. They'd, irregular free -frawls into the saloons. You'd see them the next day on the street. They'd have bands around their heads and patches over their eyes. Some of them had their teeth knocked out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And the cops, the cops were brutal at that time. Old Joe Davis and Tom Hopkins, they were the cops. And they had a cane, and they had a ball about that big around out of brass on the end of their canes. And they'd see some of these old jacks walking up the street were drunk. They'd slip up behind them and they'd rack them with this cane, this knob. Knock them out cold. Puttin' there, kill them. Blood'd be spurtin' and they'd get ahold of them, drag them up and put them into a little, well it's six feet wide and eight feet long and about six feet high. And then there's a little square window like that, about that high. There's five inches.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And I've heard those fellas as a kid, I'd stand there by the window lots of times. And listen, I'm begging for a drink of water. And we'd get a water and pour it through the outfit and they'd take a drink. And they never had no soogins on the floor at all or no toilet on the inside. They'd just go in one corner and they was worse than beast. That's the reason I had such a horror of those things at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well would these cells be inside the jail or would they be outside right on the back?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Just along the side of the street. It was just a building, six feet wide and about eight feet long. They'd put them in there, no soogins or anything, and they'd keep them overnight. They'd have to lay down on the floor and sleep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd have a bunch of these, one for each guy?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, no, just the one. One for, yeah. The one for half a dozen of them. And then later on, they built a jail because they had a problem in keeping them. And Brown built a building, oh, probably 50 feet square brick and then they made iron cells. Then they'd put them in there and they held the court and everything right in the jail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This idea of five guys in a little box like that, there probably wasn't room for any of them to lie down or nothing?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: No, no, that's what I say, it was worse than cattle.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Old Joe Davis, he'd sneak up, he was a big man too. He'd sneak up behind them and he'd wrap them over the head before they had a chance to say a word or anything else. In the saloon, lots of times the saloon keeper would come out and make them leave them alone.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: And they'd take them in, they'd promise to take them off of the street and they'd take them into a back room and they had sawdust all over the floor and they'd let them lay down on the sawdust and sleep off their jags.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the idea there that they didn't want anybody that was drunk walking on the streets?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh yes, yes. It gets a lot to be out on the street drunk and that's why they took advantage of it.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd wrap them over the head and knock them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they fine too or would they just let them out the next day?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd find them. Most of the fines were only two or three dollars because they knew they didn't have any money. The saloon keepers got all their money before they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this when you were a kid and with these Palouses on it?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I heard Palouse was really quite a wild town in those areas. Yes it was. I'll tell you, I see some awful beaten ups there.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I saw them.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Fellers that had... They'd take them to the doctor and the doctor had to keep them for two or three days sometimes to get them onto their feet.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They were cruel.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I've seen them use nooks. The saloon keepers, bartenders, used nooks on them lots of times.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd hit some of those jacks and their blood had just spurt. Well you knew they had nooks on.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: They'd make them out of lead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see them?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah. They'd make them head where they could put them through and then they'd stick up about that high. When they'd hit they'd cut just like a knife.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that the climate used to be like that?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Was everything just really tough in those days or why would you have guys... Well they were tough. But were these jacks... I thought the jacks were really sort of a harmless bunch. Well, in later years they were.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: But the jacks wasn't hurting a thing. They'd get drunk. They'd walk up the street. They wasn't bothering anybody. But these cops were the brutal ones.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Maybe that's where lots of the people got so they hated the cops. Just because they was a brutal.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: I know I've heard quite a few people mention the red light district they had in Palouse. That went along with the saloons. Yeah, they had three big houses there. I used to sell gardens, vegetables, radishes, cucumbers, tomatoes. Stuff I'd fill up my little red wagon and I'd go over there and they'd buy the whole load. The other kids at Towne had finally found out. Boy, you had to get up early in the morning and get there before they... But they'd buy the whole load, you know. Go over there with these little wagons. Oh, I had a wagon. Probably three feet long. Then the sides had sides up on it. I had to wash my radishes and cucumbers. Then I had a white cloth. I'd run over the sides and lay them all in there. Then I'd put a wet cloth over the top so none of the dust would get on. I'd light out and I'd go up to these houses. They all had Chinese cooks. They'd come out and... They'd talk in Chinese. The girls would have to come out and do the buying because I couldn't understand the Chinese. But they'd take the whole load. Of course, it's a dull amount, probably a dollar for the whole load. When the rest of the kids found out where I was selling my outfits, they got to go in there, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of those jacks actually go and live in the houses or stay there for quite a while?

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Yeah, they...

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
words: Until their money got gone and they'd kick them out.

speaker: Byers Sanderson or John Sanderson
